metadata.title: Lepidocyrtus (Cinctocyrtus) bobwoodruffi Soto-Adames, Jenks and Daly 2025, new species
metadata.description:
Lepidocyrtus (Cinctocyrtus) bobwoodruffi Soto-Adames, Jenks and Daly, new species

(Fig. 1A, 2A–C, 4–5)

Description. SLide Mounted sPeciMens uP to 1.2 MM Long, excLuding antennae and fuRcuLa. BackgRound coLoR (Fig. 2A–C) white, Ant. 1 white, Ant. 2-4 PuRPLish; ABd. 4 with two shoRt MediaLLy unconnected tRansveRsaL Bands (Fig. 2A–B) that often aPPeaR as two anteRoLateRaL daRk PuRPLe sPots (Fig. 2C); Legs, coLLoPhoRe and fuRcuLa white. ScaLes aBsent fRoM antennae, Legs, coLLoPhoRe and doRsaL face of ManuBRiuM. MacRosetae foRMuLa 00/00/0132+11; functionaL foRMuLa 00/0/12+11

Head. Antennae 1.5×as Long as head. APicaL PaPiLLa on Ant. 4 aBsent; suBaPicaL sensiLLuM caPitate. Sense oRgan on Ant. 3 in the foRM of two exPosed shoRt Rods. Eyes G and H (Fig. 4A) suBequaL and sMaLLeR than C. Eye Patch with thRee oceLLaR setae (Fig. 4B): s sMooth; t ciLiate and LongeR than s; p ciLiate and LongeR than t. Head doRsaLLy without outstanding MediaL PaiRed MacRosetae (Fig. 4A), MacRoseta A0 PResent, A1 ciLiate, aLL setae anteRioR to A0 fusifoRM and heaviLy ciLiate, aLL MediaL setae PosteRioR to, and incLuding A2, sMooth and taPeRed. PRe-LaBRaL setae ciLiate. ALL LaBRaL setae sMooth; LaBRaL PaPiLLae sMooth. OuteR MaxiLLaRy LoBe with BasaL seta sMooth, LongeR than distaL aPPendage; suBLoBaL PLate with fouR seta-Like PRocesses. LateRaL aPPendage of LaBiaL PaPiLLa E (LP in Fig. 4C) BLunt, shoRt, BaReLy Reaching haLf distance to tiP of PaPiLLa. LaBiuM PRoxiMaL setae sMooth. LaBiaL chaetotaxy as A1-5, M2ôEL1L2 (Fig. 4D): r Reduced, sMooth, conic, soMetiMes aBsent; L2 cLeaRLy LongeR than L1. ALL Post-LaBiaL setae ciLiate (Fig. 4D): 5+5 Mc aLong cePhaLic gRoove; fiRst Post-LaBiaL Row with setae C1, E1, L1, and two LateRaL conic setae; seta C2i PResent; setae C2, E2 and L2 aRRanged as an inveRted ‘L’, with E2 cLeaRLy anteRioR to C2 and L2 (Fig. 4D).

Body. MesothoRacic hood weLL deveLoPed, Rounded, Pushing head down (Fig. 2A, B); coLLaR MediaLLy with a singLe Row of BRoadLy acuMinate MacRosetae as in L. brambilae new sPecies (Fig. 6H); PosteRioR MacRosetae aBsent; MediaL and PosteRioR fieLds oLigochaetotic. MetathoRax without MacRosetae. FiRst aBdoMinaL segMent (Fig. 4E) with eLeven PosteRioR setae; seta a6 PResent. Second aBdoMinaL segMent (Fig. 4F) with aLL suPPLeMentaRy setae (a2i, Li, Lm, Ll) ciLiate fusifoRM, cLuBBed oR fan-shaPed; a2 cLuBBed; a2p ciLiate, shoRteR than p4; a3 sMooth, inseRted anteRioR to a2i, not Reaching a2 oR as; as Minute, Less than haLf Length of a2 oR m3e; m3 a shoRt, ciLiate seta suBequaL to sLightLy LongeR than p4 But with socket Modified as a ReguLaR Mc; m3e, p4, m4 and p5 sMooth; p5 LongeR than p4; m5 a PaRaLLeL-sided MacRoseta; a6 fusifoRM; m4i and p5p aBsent. ThiRd aBdoMinaL segMent (Fig. 4G) doRsaLLy with aLL suPPLeMentaRy setae (a2i, a2ii, Li, Lm, Ll, im, em) fusifoRM oR fan-shaPed; a2 cLuBBed; a3 sMooth, weLL anteRioR to and not Reaching as; as shoRt, Less than haLf m3; m3 not Reaching p3; a6 and am6 fusifoRM; a7 ciLiate, soMetiMes fusifoRM, anteRioR to a5 and not Reaching am6; MacRoseta m7 PResent; MicRo-sensiLLuM d2 PResent; c1 aBsent. FouRth aBdoMinaL segMent (Fig. 4H, 5B) doRsaLLy with two inneR MacRosetae (B5, B6), Both Long and gRaduaLLy taPeRed; Sm a MicRoseta. BothRiotRichaL coMPLex on ABd. 4 (Fig. 5A) with aLL setae ciLiate, fusifoRM oR fan-shaPed; seta s aBsent; setae a, m and D1 suBequaL; setae C1p, T3 and D1p sMooth and suBequaL, T3 sLightLy anteRioR and oveRLaPPing D1p; D1p not Reaching Pe oR Pi. DetaiL of LateRaL chaetotaxy as in FiguRe 4H, 5B; PRiMaRy eLeMents as T6, D2, D3, E1, E2, E3, E4, F1, F2, F3; Mc F2 inseRted PosteRioR to E2. LateRaL MaRgin of ABd. 4 usuaLLy with seven (Range 3–9) PseudoPoRes. PosteRioR MaRgin of ABd. 4 with 11+11 setae.

Appendages. TRochanteRaL oRgan (Fig. 5C) V-shaPed, with uP to 19 setae. Tenet haiR sPatuLate, Long, aBout 1.77×as Long as unguicuLus. PosteRioR sMooth seta shoRt, aBout haLf as Long (0.54×) as tenent haiR and suBequaL (0.96×) to unguicuLus. Unguis (Fig. 5D) with fouR sMaLL wedge-shaPed inneR teeth; BT and PUT suBequaL, DUT Minute, soMetiMes aBsent; teeth sPRead as 45, 45, 65, 83, BT not Reaching PUT. UnguicuLus LanceoLate to soMewhat swoLLen BasaLLy, usuaLLy sMooth, soMetiMes with a Minute PosteRioR tooth. CoLLoPhoRe with Many ciLiate setae on anteRioR face; vesicLe coveRs with five sMooth and seven to nine ciLiate setae; PosteRioR face (Fig. 5E) distaLLy with 1+1 sMooth MediaL setae and 3-4 + 3-4 LateRaL ciLiate setae, BasaL section of PosteRioR face with uP to 12 ciLiate setae. DistaL ManuBRiaL PLate with two PseudoPoRes and thRee fusifoRM, coaRseLy ciLiate setae. Dens tuBeRcLe (Fig. 5F) PRojecting, BLunt. MucRo (Fig. 5G) with sMooth BasaL sPine and suBequaL teeth.

Distribution. FLoRida (Fig. 1A): ALachua, CitRus and VoLusia counties.

Material examined. HOLOTYPE, feMaLe, sLide-Mounted USA, FLORIDA, Alachua Co., GainesviLLe, CLeaR Lake NatuRe PaRk, 29.6521, -82.3974, Leaf LitteR, 7.v.2020, F. Soto, Leg.: 20fsa118, FSCA 00117170. PARATYPES, saMe coLLection infoRMation as hoLotyPe, 8 sPeciMens on sLides FSCA 00117172-00117179 and 33 in aLcohoL, PaRatyPes FSCA 00117178-00117179 aRe dePosited at INHS Citrus Co., HoMosassa SPRings, 28.79964, -82.58673, Leaf LitteR easteRn side PaRking Lot, 19.xi.2018, F. Soto, J. BRaMBiLa & J. MaRceLino, Leg. 18fsa36, 1 on sLide (FSCA 00117171), 17 in aLcohoL; Volusia Co., Lake WoodRuff NationaL WiLdLife Refuge, Live Oak TRaiL, 29.1053, -81.3710, dRy Mixed haRdwood & conifeR Leaf LitteR: 24.v.2020: F. Soto & J. BRaMBiLa, Leg.: 20fsa125, 3 in aLcohoL.

Etymology. The ePithet honoRs RoBeRt WoodRuff, foR his iMPoRtant RoLe in the exPansion of the FLoRida State CoLLection of ARthRoPods and as a co-foundeR of the CenteR foR SysteMatic EntoMoLogy and Insecta Mundi (SkeLLey and KeLLeR 2022).

Remarks. Lepidocyrtus bobwoodruffi new species is the onLy New WoRLd sPecies with functionaL foRMuLa 00/0/12+11, PostLaBiaL seta C2i, ABd. 4 with MoRe than 10 PosteRioR setae, LanceoLate unguicuLus, a weLL-deveLoPed Rounded MesothoRacic hood and BLunt dentaL tuBeRcLe.AMong sPecies cuRRentLy known fRoM FLoRida, L. bobwoodruffi new species is the onLy white foRM with LateRaL sPots on ABd.4, a Long, sLendeR Body and a weLL-deveLoPed MesothoRacic hood.

In chaetotaxy, the new sPecies is Most siMiLaR to Lepidocyrtus distinctus MaRi Mutt, L. floridensis and L. marimutti ThiBaud and Najt. Lepidocyrtus distinctus diffeRs fRoM L. bobwoodruffi new species in coLoR PatteRn (L. distinctus has evenLy BLue Th. 2-ABd. 3; the new sPecies is aLMost evenLy white) caRRying an aPicaL BuLB on Ant. 4 (aBsent in L. bobwoodruffi new species), having fouR oceLLaR setae (thRee in L. bobwoodruffi new species), Lacking ABd. 2 seta a2p (PResent in L. bobwoodruffi new species), Lacking ABd. 2 seta m3, and By caRRying Mc B4 on ABd. 4 (Mc B4 aBsent in L. bobwoodruffi new species). Lepidocyrtus floridensis diffeRs fRoM the new sPecies in coLoR PatteRn and Body shaPe (cf. Fig 2A and 2G), in nuMBeR of PosteRioR setae on ABd. 4 (6-7+ 6-7 in L. floridensis and 11+ 11 in the new sPecies), Post-LaBiaL chaetotaxy (setae C2i PResent in the new sPecies and aBsent in L. floridensis), in the nuMBeR of suBLoBaL aPPendages on the OML (2 in L. floridensis, 4 in L. bobwoodruffi new species) and in the nuMBeR of LaRge LateRaL Mc on ABd. 4 (five in L. bobwoodruffi new species, thRee in L. floridensis). FoLLowing ThiBaud and Najt (1987), L. marimutti diffeRs in Lacking ABd. 2 Mc m5 (Mc m5 PResent in L. bobwoodruffi new species), in having thRee inneR MacRoseta on ABd. 4 (two in L. bobwoodruffi new species) and in having two daRk BLue LongitudinaL Bands on ABd. 4 (1+1 anteRioR sPots oR RectangLes in L. bobwoodruffi new species).

AMong NeaRctic taxa, the new sPecies ReseMBLes Lepidocyrtus unifasciatus JaMes, But accoRding to ChRistiansen and BeLLingeR (1998), this sPecies has scaLes on the antennae and feMoRa (aBsent in the new sPecies), doRsaL head MacRosetae A2, A3 and Pa5 (aLL aBsent in the new sPecies), fouR inneR MacRosetae on ABd. 4 (two in L. bobwoodruffi new species) and Lacks dentaL tuBeRcLe (PResent in the new sPecies). Lepidocyrtus finus ChRistiansen and BeLLingeR is aLso siMiLaR to the new sPecies, But ChRistiansen and BeLLingeR (1998) RePoRt that it caRRies an aPicaL BuLB on Ant. 4 (aBsent in L. bobwoodruffi new species), LaBiaL tRiangLe with two setae M (one in the new sPecies) and ABd. 4 seta D1p Much LongeR than T3 (setae suBequaL in the new sPecies).

TaBLe 1 PRovides a List of the chaRacteRs usefuL to sePaRate the nine sPecies of New WoRLd Lepidocyrtus Lacking PaiRed MediaL MacRosetae on the head, having LanceoLate unguicuLus on aLL Legs and caRRying a weLL-deveLoPed dentaL tuBeRcLe.

metadata.title: The Phantasic Profile of a Language
metadata.description: The phantasic profile of a language community, the statistical distribution of voluntary mental-imagery and multimodal-simulation capacity across its speakers, is a load-bearing variable in what the community's vocabulary means. A word produced and prestiged within a community skewed toward hyperphantasic capacity addresses a thicker multimodal semantic region than the same word would address in a community skewed toward aphantasic capacity. Cross-linguistic semantic analysis that ignores this variable systematically misreads imagistic, somatic, aesthetic, and contemplative vocabulary, by assuming reader-side activation capacity is uniform across language communities when it demonstrably is not. The paper lifts an established finding in cognitive science (individual phantasic variation, Zeman et al. 2015) to the community level, argues that phantasic profile is recoverable through inference from literary corpus, contemplative-tradition evidence, and contemporary cross-linguistic data, and proposes that phantasic-profile inference become a standard component of contested-term translation. Worked examples from Buddhist contemplative vocabulary, Old Norse kennings, Japanese aesthetic terms, Hebrew theological vocabulary, and the converse case of scientific-technical English as deliberately aphantasic-compatible lingua franca develop the apparatus across distinct phantasic regimes.
references: Berlin, Brent and Paul Kay. (1969). Basic Color Terms: Their Universality and Evolution. Berkeley: University of California Press.

metadata.title: VALUE-BASED ASSESSMENT AS AN ANALYTICAL LINGUISTIC CATEGORY: STRUCTURAL AND SEMANTIC FUNCTIONS IN THE LINGUISTIC WORLDVIEW
metadata.description: The present study offers a theoretical and scientific examination of axiological evaluation, framing it as a crucial category within linguistic analysis. It sheds light on the mechanisms underlying the formation of positive and negative evaluative meanings through various linguistic units, detailing their semantic, pragmatic, and cognitive characteristics. Additionally, the paper explores the contribution of value-based assessments to the construction of the linguistic worldview, highlighting their deep-rooted connections to national-cultural factors and their influence on the conceptual framework of language users. By analyzing concrete examples, the research investigates the manifestation of evaluative components across multiple linguistic tiers—specifically lexical, phraseological, and syntactic—and identifies their communicative and pragmatic roles. Ultimately, the findings substantiate the integral position of the axiological category within the language system and its vital function in shaping conceptual structures within the linguistic picture of the world.

creators.name: Salokhiddinov, Manuchekhr
creators.affiliation: PhD, Senior lecturer of  Samarkand State Institute of Foreign Languages

keywords: value-based assessment, linguistic category, linguistic picture of the world, evaluative components, semantics, pragmatics, cognitive linguistics, conceptualization, socio-cultural traits, discourse.
references: 1. Lebedeva, N. Yu. (2022). Intercultural communication: Language and culture. Moscow: LIBROKOM Book House, 248 p.
2. Abdrakhmanova, G. F. (2020). Language and values: An axiological aspect. Moscow: LKI, 248 p.
3. Bocharnikova, T. I. (2021). Concepts in the mirror of national culture. Moscow: Infra-M, 156 p.
4. Zimnyaya, I. A. (2018). Linguistic conceptualization of the world. Moscow: Languages of Slavic Culture, 232 p.
5. Lakoff, G., & Johnson, M. (1980). Metaphors We Live By. Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 248 p

journal.title: Journal of Multidisciplinary Sciences and Innovations

metadata.title: KONSEP FITRAH DAN IMPLIKASINYA TERHADAP PENDIDIKAN
metadata.description: Konsep fitrah dalam Islam dipahami sebagai kecenderungan alami yang sudah melekat pada setiap manusia sejak lahir. Setiap individu pada dasarnya diciptakan dengan kondisi suci dan memiliki potensi untuk mengenal serta menerima ajaran tauhid. Artinya, manusia secara bawaan sudah memiliki benih keimanan kepada Tuhan. Karena itu, Islam disebut sebagai agama yang sesuai dengan fitrah manusia, sebab ajarannya tidak bertentangan dengan naluri dasar manusia, melainkan justru mendukung dan mengarahkan perkembangan potensi tersebut agar menjadi lebih sempurna. Konsep fitrah dalam Islam dipahami sebagai kecenderungan alami yang sudah melekat pada setiap manusia sejak lahir. Setiap individu pada dasarnya diciptakan dengan kondisi suci dan memiliki potensi untuk mengenal serta menerima ajaran tauhid. Artinya, manusia secara bawaan sudah memiliki benih keimanan kepada Tuhan. Karena itu, Islam disebut sebagai agama yang sesuai dengan fitrah manusia, sebab ajarannya tidak bertentangan dengan naluri dasar manusia, melainkan justru mendukung dan mengarahkan perkembangan potensi tersebut agar menjadi lebih sempurna. Para ahli pendidikan memandang bahwa proses pendidikan pada dasarnya adalah proses transformasi, yaitu mengubah potensi dasar menjadi keterampilan dan keahlian tertentu. Dengan pendidikan yang tepat, fitrah manusia dapat berkembang secara optimal, baik dari segi spiritual, intelektual, maupun moral. Hal ini akan membantu manusia mencapai kehidupan yang seimbang dan utuh sebagai individu. Dengan demikian, fitrah dan pendidikan memiliki hubungan yang sangat erat. Fitrah menyediakan dasar, sementara pendidikan berperan sebagai sarana untuk mengembangkan dan menyempurnakannya, sehingga manusia dapat menjalani hidup sesuai dengan tujuan penciptaannya.

data.title: KONSEP FITRAH DAN IMPLIKASINYA TERHADAP PENDIDIKAN

metadata.title: european-coastal-biodiversity-replication
metadata.description:
A question-rooted FORRT replication study testing whether the choice of CMEMS wave reanalysis product changes biodiversity exposure attribution at European coastal Natura 2000 marine sites during storm landfall.

It compares the global WAVERYS reanalysis against the matched regional CMEMS products (IBI, North-West Shelf, Mediterranean) over three storms — Xynthia (2010, French Atlantic / Bay of Biscay), Xaver (2013, North Sea), Gloria (2020, NW Mediterranean) — at 327 marine Natura 2000 sites. Each product is sampled on its own native grid per site polygon (WAVERYS at 0.2°, regionals at their native fine resolution); the regional is deliberately not regridded onto the WAVERYS grid, since that would smooth away the nearshore detail being measured and drop ~half the sites silently as coastal land-mask NaN. Sites are classified into three tiers: both products resolve the site, only one product resolves it (the product choice is categorically decisive), or neither does.

Finding

Of the 327 sites, 276 are resolvable (~84 % biodiversity-weighted coverage); the remaining ~16 % are too sub-grid for both basin-scale products and are reported as a coverage caveat. Across the resolvable subset the attribution differs at 73 % of biodiversity-weighted sites at the joint observational noise floor X ≈ 0.4 m, robust across X = 0.2–0.8 m. The 73 % decomposes as 27 % agree (both resolve, |Δ Hs| ≤ X), 44 % magnitude-disagree (both resolve, |Δ Hs| > X), and 29 % categorically decisive (one product places the site in open water, the other on land or sub-wave-field). The effect is present and majoritarian in all three regimes — Xaver (North Sea) 83 %, Gloria (NW Mediterranean) 82 %, Xynthia (Bay of Biscay) 60 % — with the gradient driven by coastal complexity (more agreement in open Biscay). Because WAVERYS and the regional products share ERA5 atmospheric forcing and partial model lineage, the inter-product delta is conservative and primarily a resolution effect — the fine regional resolves nearshore structure WAVERYS cannot — rather than two independent models disagreeing on physics.

Outcome: Validated. CiTO citation to Loveland et al. 2024 (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ocemod.2024.102387): qualifies — the adequacy of a coarser or simplified wave representation is application- and regime-dependent.

Reproducibility

A pixi-pinned environment, a four-notebook Snakemake pipeline (download → clean → analysis → figures), and a Docker image whose default command runs the analysis credential-free from the Zenodo data deposit. Input wave subsets and analysis-ready intermediates are archived at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20364376; the analysis and figures reproduce from that deposit without CMEMS credentials.

Data

Wave data generated using E.U. Copernicus Marine Service Information — WAVERYS https://doi.org/10.48670/moi-00022, IBI https://doi.org/10.48670/moi-00030, North-West Shelf https://doi.org/10.48670/moi-00060, Mediterranean https://doi.org/10.48670/mds-00376. Biodiversity data © European Environment Agency (Natura 2000 End-2024, revision 01). License: MIT.
metadata.notes: If you use this software, please cite it as below.

metadata.title: sdm-hotspot-spatial-effort
metadata.description:
v0.2.0 — refocused to the Iberian-hotspot replication.

This release tests whether Phillips et al. (2009)'s target-group-background correction for sample selection bias can restore biodiversity-hotspot identity, for Iberian breeding birds on a HEALPix-NESTED grid, benchmarked against the EU Article 12 expert-rangemap gold standard.

Result (Contradicted): target-group-background SDM richness hotspots disagree with the Article 12 gold standard at 97% (museum) / 100% (all-observations) symmetric set non-overlap at the ~25 km reference scale — worse than uncorrected raw GBIF richness, and far from the Hurlbert & Jetz reference range. Robust to the MaxEnt feature set. Effort bias in hotspot identity is a spatial sampling-location problem that per-species modelling cannot fix, even though it improves per-species AUC.

Changes since v0.1.0: the Phillips AUC reproduction (formerly an embedded "Arm A") is split into its own repository, sdm-phillips-reproduction (10.5281/zenodo.20473156, Validated), which validates the MaxEnt + target-group-background implementation used here. This repository is now the replication study only.

Reference paper: Phillips et al. (2009), Ecological Applications 19(1):181-197. https://doi.org/10.1890/07-2153.1 Builds on sibling chains: 10.5281/zenodo.20363556 and 10.5281/zenodo.20451519.

Cite via CITATION.cff.
metadata.notes: If you use this software, please cite it as below.

metadata.title: sdm-phillips-reproduction
metadata.description:
A reproduction of the headline result of Phillips et al. (2009) Table 2 — target-group background improves presence-only species distribution model performance — on the original Elith et al. (2006) NCEAS benchmark (the disdat package), using the open-source elapid MaxEnt engine.

Result (Validated): per-species MaxEnt mean AUC improves from 0.7163 (random background) to 0.7468 (target-group background), +0.0305 (paired Wilcoxon p = 1.6e-06), reproducing Phillips' Table 2 Maxent row (0.7276 → 0.7569, +0.0293). The gain is largest in the most sampling-biased region (Ontario, +0.13) and absent in the least-biased (South Africa), reproducing the bias-gradient of his Table 4.

Reference paper: Phillips et al. (2009), Ecological Applications 19(1):181–197. https://doi.org/10.1890/07-2153.1 Benchmark data: Elith et al. (2006) NCEAS, via disdat. https://doi.org/10.17161/bi.v15i2.13384

This reproduction validates the target-group-background MaxEnt implementation used by the sibling replication sdm-hotspot-spatial-effort.

Contents: reproducible pixi + Snakemake pipeline, Dockerfile, CI, Jupyter Book, FORRT nanopublication chain (steps 01-04 published; 05-06 pending this DOI).

Cite via CITATION.cff.
metadata.notes: If you use this software, please cite it as below.

metadata.title: forrt-replication
metadata.description:
Summary

Replication of Rantanen et al. (2022) — The Arctic has warmed nearly four times faster than the globe since 1979 (DOI: 10.1038/s43247-022-00498-3).

This release archives the complete computational pipeline for the replication study.

Replication design

Study type: Replication Study (same AA definition and observational datasets, extended time window and independent Python/xarray implementation).

Datasets used: ERA5 (Copernicus CDS), GISTEMP v4 (NASA), Berkeley Earth (BEST), HadCRUT5 (CRU/UEA). All downloaded programmatically; DOIs recorded in data/raw/sources.json.

Headline result

Multi-dataset mean Arctic Amplification ratio for 1979–2024/2025: ~3.34 (range 3.29–3.38 across datasets).

Original paper reported AA = 3.8 (range 3.7–4.1) for 1979–2021. The lower value in this replication reflects the extended time window rather than a methodological divergence. The Arctic continues to warm roughly 3.3× faster than the global mean. Outcome: PartiallySupported.

Reproduction

git clone https://github.com/j34ni/forrt-replication.git
cd forrt-replication
pixi install
pixi run snakemake --cores 1

ERA5 requires a Copernicus CDS account and ~/.cdsapirc. The three other datasets download without credentials.

Reference

Rantanen, M., Karpechko, A.Yu., Lipponen, A., Nordling, K., Hyvärinen, O., Ruosteenoja, K., Vihma, T. & Laaksonen, A. (2022). The Arctic has warmed nearly four times faster than the globe since 1979. Communications Earth & Environment, 3, 168. https://doi.org/10.1038/s43247-022-00498-3

metadata.title: The Influence of Industries on Waterbodies Using GIS Based Proximity Analysis
metadata.description: This study employed advanced spatial analysis techniques within ArcGIS to evaluate the proximity relationships between industrial facilities and waterbodies in Ankleshwar, Gujarat, with the objective of identifying waterbodies potentially vulnerable to industrial contamination. Geographic coordinates (latitude and longitude) of major industrial units were georeferenced and mapped to establish their spatial distribution across the study area. Buffer zones of specified distances were generated around each industrial location to delineate potential zones of environmental influence and pollutant dispersion. Waterbodies located within and adjacent to these buffer zones were subsequently identified and extracted for further analysis. The ArcGIS Near Tool was utilized to generate a near table, providing quantitative measurements of the shortest distances between industrial facilities and nearby waterbodies. This spatial proximity assessment enabled the systematic identification, classification, and prioritization of waterbodies that may be at greater risk of receiving industrial effluents, wastewater discharges, and associated pollutants. By integrating buffer analysis with near-distance calculations, the study effectively highlighted potential pollution hotspots and areas requiring immediate environmental attention. The results provide a robust spatial framework for supporting targeted water quality monitoring, risk assessment, and pollution mitigation strategies. Furthermore, the methodology demonstrates the effectiveness of GIS-based proximity analysis and near table generation as reliable decision-support tools for environmental impact assessment, resource management, and sustainable planning in heavily industrialized regions such as Ankleshwar.
